title: Cock-a-leekie
subtitle: The Scottish national soup ,  chicken, leeks, and prunes
excerpt: Scotland's national soup: a clear, golden chicken broth filled with leeks and, traditionally, stoned prunes. The prunes sound unlikely; they balance the savoury depth of the broth and soften to nothing in the bowl.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861) ,  Project Gutenberg #10136. Cock-a-leekie entry with and without prunes.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845) ,  Project Gutenberg. Scottish leek soup method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the broth and chicken separately from the prunes. Cool completely and freeze for up to three months. Add fresh prunes when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The soup improves overnight. Make the full pot the day before and skim any fat from the surface before reheating.

glossaryTerms.definition: Scotland's national soup, traditionally made with an old boiling fowl ('cock') and leeks. The name is a Scots English compound of the two main ingredients.

content.text: is Scotland's national soup: a clear golden broth from a long-simmered chicken, loaded with soft leeks and the surprising addition of prunes. The prunes are traditional and, despite appearances, essential. They bring a gentle sweetness and a slight tartness that rounds out the savoury depth of the broth in a way that nothing else quite does.

items.prepNote: white and pale green parts, sliced into 1 cm rounds

content.text: Place the {{chicken-whole}} whole chicken in a large stockpot with the {{onion}} onion, {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves, and {{thyme}} of thyme sprigs. Cover with 2.5 litres of cold water. Bring slowly to a simmer, skimming the surface as foam appears.
Add {{pearl-barley}} of pearl barley. Simmer gently for 1 hour.
Lift the chicken out and set aside. Add half the sliced {{leek}} leeks and all the {{prunes}} prunes to the broth. Simmer for 20 minutes.
Pull the chicken meat from the carcass, shred, and add the best pieces back to the pot. Discard the bones and skin.
Add the remaining leek slices. Simmer for 5 minutes until just tender. Remove the bay leaves and thyme. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into deep bowls, making sure each portion has some chicken, leek, and at least one prune. Scatter flat-leaf parsley over the top.

items.symptom: Prunes have dissolved into the broth
items.cause: They were added too early and simmered too long.
items.fix: Add the prunes with the second batch of leeks, not at the start. They need about 20 minutes, not 90.

items.cause: The chicken was boiled at too high a heat.
items.fix: Always start with cold water and never let the pot boil hard. A gentle simmer keeps the broth clear.

items.cause: Barley takes at least 45 minutes to cook properly.
items.fix: Add it at the very start with the chicken, not later. An extra 15 minutes of cooking won't hurt it.

items.cause: The chicken was too small or the simmering time was too short.
items.fix: Allow a full hour at minimum. The broth needs time to develop. A small knob of butter stirred in before serving adds richness to a thin broth.

content.text: Cock-a-leekie appears in Scottish records from at least the sixteenth century. The prunes are documented in recipes from the early seventeenth century, when sweet and savoury were more freely combined than they are now. The soup is traditionally served at Burns Night suppers alongside the haggis. Victorian cookbooks listed two versions: one with prunes, one without. The prunes were considered essential then. They still are.
